body_txt: Some people are won over by sincerity, others by flattery, while others still are convinced by someone displaying knowledge and/or skill. Some, however, like a good old bout of chutzpah. Perhaps this is because this level of audacity requires a degree of confidence. After all, it has been said that a person who is convinced of their defeat won't be able to win. But this person is not only convinced they won't lose, they often believe they can win handily with a bit of gumption, hard work, and a bit of ingenuity. They could be, or inspire, The Determinator , a Blood Knight , or a Proud Warrior Race Guy . They'll be the ones to seek Refuge in Audacity . When they're seemingly hopelessly outnumbered, they might be the one to tell their enemy, "You've got me outnumbered 100 to 1. I Like Those Odds ." They may also subscribe to Cunning People Play Poker , and believe in a good bluff when they don't hold the cards. Conversely, they may be prone to Brutal Honesty . Their gumption may win over someone offering a Secret Test of Character . In more mundane settings, it could result in a Hire the Critic moment. The one who shows such chutzpah will generally be of the optimistic, spirited sort, possibly even being Hot-Blooded . And their confidence and audaciousness in the face of what others might see as overwhelming odds inspire others to side with them. Don't tell them Do Not Taunt Cthulhu . They'll be the ones flipping him the bird , sitting down for a nice cup of tea with him , or even wooing him ! If the person they're displaying this audacious confidence to is an enemy, they will either view them as a Worthy Opponent , or see them as a complete and utter idiot (though if they take them lightly, they might find that they're dealing with a Crouching Moron, Hidden Badass ). If the foe is actively insulted, they may think it Crosses the Line Twice and/or find it Actually Pretty Funny . If it doesn't prompt a Heel–Face Turn , it might at least earn a sincere pledge not to interfere while the hero deals with a Greater-Scope Villain . They may be told " You Got Guts ", though this can just as easily be said by someone who is annoyed with them and ready to strike back, e.g., "You've got guts, kid. And I'm about to spill 'em for ya." The key element lies in the display of audacity being an act or a response that, crucially, would be seen as blatantly rude, offensive, disrespectful, or worthy of condemnation in any other circumstance. But the offending character, instead of picking up their teeth with broken fingers, gets a pat on the back instead. Why? Because the other party, who would otherwise react negatively, admires the gall it takes to go through with the stunt in the first place, regardless of the potential consequences for doing so. This often makes them a Magnetic Hero . In other words, it's a situation where respect for audacity explicitly outweighs the severity or rudeness of the affront (whereas Refuge in Audacity relies on exploiting incredulity to get away with wrongdoing). It can lead to the offending character being rewarded or spared from punishment, but not always. For this to be in effect, the person must be won over by a display of audacious bravado. Could also be referred to as "moxie", "gumption", "spirit", and "spunk". When a character's praise is subtle, it would be considered Not Bad . Compare and contrast Actually Pretty Impressive , where the act itself impresses, rather than the attitude to try it; Actually Pretty Funny , where the butt of a joke also finds it funny, regardless of how it portrays them; and Do Wrong, Right , where the other character does not approve of an audacious act, but for technical rather than the expected moral reasons. May overlap with Playful "Perv" Accusation , when someone ironically uses the accusation of perversions to signal their own interest.
